title: Hormuz Reopening Hits New Obstacle as IRGC Strikes Cargo Ship; Iraq Rattles OPEC and France Seizes Fifth Shadow Fleet Tanker
subhead: A cargo vessel was struck by a projectile in the Strait of Hormuz just as tanker traffic was recovering, prompting the IMO to suspend its evacuation plan and exposing the fragility of the US-Iran peace deal. Meanwhile, Iraq threatened then retracted an OPEC exit, France seized its fifth Russian shadow fleet tanker, and the US Army moved to put critical minerals processing on military bases.

sections.heading: Hormuz: IRGC Strike on Cargo Ship Halts IMO Evacuation
sections.body:
A cargo vessel transiting the Gulf of Oman was struck on its starboard side by an unidentified projectile on Thursday, sustaining damage to the bridge, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) agency. A US official told CNN the ship was hit by an Iranian drone. No casualties or environmental damage were reported.

The incident occurred within hours of several commercial vessels — including at least two oil supertankers — turning back while attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz outbound on the Oman side. Shipping intelligence firm Windward Maritime said the U-turns followed IRGC radio calls and social media directives instructing vessels to reverse course.

Iran's Persian Gulf Strait Authority reiterated that ships must obtain IRGC Navy authorization and use designated transit routes, warning that vessels outside its framework would not be eligible for insurance coverage or 'safe-passage guarantees.'

The International Maritime Organization suspended its evacuation operation for stranded seafarers hours after the strike. IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez said the program — launched June 23 to evacuate over 11,000 seafarers — would pause 'until further clarity is obtained' on safety guarantees. The IMO also canceled a scheduled press briefing citing unspecified urgent matters.

A White House official said it was too soon to attribute the attack, noting the US was investigating whether the order came from senior IRGC leadership or was a rogue action by lower-level personnel.
sections.whyItMatters: The strike and the IRGC's renewed transit controls expose how conditional the Hormuz reopening remains. Tanker traffic had been recovering since the June 17 peace deal, with roughly 20 million barrels passing through in a single day. The IMO's suspension of its evacuation framework signals that even the UN's safety guarantees are no longer considered reliable.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Fall to Pre-War Levels as Hormuz Flows Recover
sections.body:
Brent crude settled around $75 per barrel on Friday, near pre-war levels, after briefly touching session highs above $75 following the Hormuz vessel strike. WTI traded around $71.79. The price decline over recent sessions reflects the significant recovery in tanker traffic through Hormuz following the June 17 US-Iran interim peace deal, which included a 60-day toll-free passage period.

Approximately 20 million barrels of oil passed through the strait in a 24-hour period this week, according to market data. IMF PortWatch's 7-day moving average showed the probability of normalized Hormuz traffic by end of June rising to 5.2%, up from 2% the previous day — still low in absolute terms but a notable directional shift.

The rapid price decline from April's peak of $126.41 per barrel has squeezed revenues for Gulf producers while easing inflation pressure on importing nations. The easing of US sanctions on Iranian oil sales has added supply to an already rebalancing market.
sections.whyItMatters: The speed of the price collapse — from $126 to $75 in six weeks — shows how much of the premium was risk-driven rather than supply-driven. If the IRGC continues to assert transit control, the market may reprice the security discount. If Hormuz fully normalizes, prices could test the low $70s.

sections.heading: Iraq Threatens OPEC Exit, Then Walks It Back
sections.body:
Iraq sent conflicting signals on Thursday about its OPEC membership. Reuters reported that Baghdad had considered leaving the cartel if its production quota was not significantly raised, with a senior Oil Ministry official telling the news agency that the matter should be treated 'with the utmost seriousness.' Hours later, the Oil Ministry issued a formal statement denying any plans to quit OPEC, saying such reports 'did not reflect the Iraqi government's official position.'

Iraq's current OPEC quota stands at approximately 4.378 million barrels per day for July, but actual output has collapsed to roughly 1.48 million bpd in May — down from nearly 4.2 million bpd in February — due to the Hormuz disruption blocking exports. Iraq's stated production ambition is 7 million bpd.

The episode comes less than two months after the UAE exited OPEC in April 2026. OPEC+ has approved nearly 600,000 bpd in quota increases across seven core members between April and June, though most members have been unable to deliver on higher targets because of the Hormuz disruption. A Russian oil source said the Iraqi situation did not represent a major challenge for OPEC+ and that a slight quota increase may help.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq is OPEC's second-largest producer and a founding member — the cartel was formed in Baghdad in 1960. While Baghdad walked back the exit threat, the economic pressure driving it is real. If Hormuz reopens and Iraq's exports recover, its demand for a much higher quota will intensify, adding another fracture to an organization already missing the UAE.

sections.heading: France Seizes Fifth Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker Near Sicily
sections.body:
French Navy forces boarded and seized the tanker Deliver off the coast of Sicily on Tuesday, June 23, President Emmanuel Macron announced on Thursday. The vessel was sailing from Russia's Baltic port of Primorsk under a Cameroonian flag that French authorities deemed invalid — a practice known as 'flag-hopping' used by shadow fleet vessels to evade tracking.

Macron said the operation was part of a broader European effort: 'We will not allow the shadow fleet to circumvent sanctions and finance Russia's war effort.' The seizure comes days after British Royal Marines boarded and detained the tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel — the UK's first direct seizure of a Russian shadow fleet vessel.

France has now boarded five shadow fleet vessels since September 2025, including the Boracay (flagged to Benin, Chinese captain tried in absentia), the Grinch (January), and the Deyna (March, Mozambican flag, detained in Marseille). In April, France announced plans to double penalties for ships failing to fly a valid flag or refusing to comply with orders. Russia's embassy in France called the Deliver seizure 'another case of piracy.'
sections.whyItMatters: The escalation from boarding and releasing to seizure and detention marks a shift in European sanctions enforcement posture. With the UK now conducting its own operations and France on its fifth interdiction, the shadow fleet is facing sustained naval pressure in Mediterranean and Atlantic waters — though Russia's response has been diplomatic condemnation rather than retaliatory action.

sections.heading: Moscow Refinery Offline Until 2027 as Russia Rations Fuel
sections.body:
The Moscow Oil Refinery, operated by Gazprom Neft, is unlikely to resume production before 2027 after Ukrainian drone strikes knocked out over 53% of its processing capacity, according to Reuters. The facility, which processed 11.6 million metric tons of crude in 2024 and supplies nearly half of the Moscow region's fuel, was struck twice in a single week in mid-June.

The shutdown has worsened an existing Russian fuel crisis. Weekly gasoline output dropped nearly 25%, and roughly 10 regions have introduced fuel rationing, with purchase limits typically capped at 30-40 liters per vehicle. Crimea has imposed the strictest restrictions, limiting public service operating hours.

Russia's parliament on Wednesday approved a package of measures to address shortages, including subsidies for imported fuel and temporary relief for refiners. Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak confirmed the government is weighing a complete ban on diesel exports and considering fuel imports from Turkey and Asian suppliers. Russia's strategic fuel reserves have also been targeted — a Ukrainian strike on the Kombinat Temp depot in Rybinsk destroyed 15 tactical fuel tanks.
sections.whyItMatters: The Moscow refinery's extended outage transforms what was a temporary disruption into a sustained domestic fuel crisis. The potential diesel export ban would tighten global middle distillate supply at a moment when Hormuz is recovering — offsetting some of the price relief from increased crude flows.

sections.heading: US Army Awards First Critical Minerals Contracts on Military Bases
sections.body:
The US Army announced provisional contracts to four companies to build critical minerals processing plants on American military installations — the first time commercial mineral processing facilities will be sited on US military bases.

REalloys Inc. will construct a rare-earth separation facility at Tooele Army Depot in Utah, with production stockpiled on-site for military use. Titan Mining Corp. will build a graphite purification facility at either Pine Bluff Arsenal in Arkansas or Anniston Army Depot in Alabama. EnergyX will develop a lithium processing facility, and Sydney-based ioneer Ltd. — the only non-US company — will build a boron plant.

The agreements are structured as Enhanced Use Leases: in lieu of cash payments, the lessee funds and executes infrastructure improvements. Construction is expected to begin as early as 2027, with mineral production by 2028. REalloys raised $100 million in a private placement on Wednesday. Titan Mining shares rose as much as 12% on the news.

The move follows a broader Trump administration push: last week China placed export controls on US rare earth companies MP Materials and USA Rare Earth; the G7 set a target to reduce reliance on Chinese mineral imports; and the Department of War's Office of Strategic Capital extended $725 million in conditional loans to Energy Fuels and $500 million to Phoenix Tailings. In February, the government announced plans for a $12 billion critical minerals stockpile.
sections.whyItMatters: Putting mineral processing plants inside military bases is a physical expression of the US national security argument for supply chain independence. It bypasses NIMBY opposition and gives the Pentagon direct access to processed materials. But 2028 production timelines mean this does not address near-term dependence on Chinese rare earths — the gap is being managed with financial instruments, not operational capacity.

bottomLine: The Hormuz reopening suffered its first serious reversal on Thursday: an IRGC-linked strike on a cargo ship and renewed Iranian transit controls prompted the IMO to suspend its seafarer evacuation plan and sent at least two supertankers turning back. Oil prices briefly spiked but settled near pre-war levels around $75, reflecting the market's view that the disruption is episodic rather than structural. Iraq's 12-hour threat to quit OPEC — quickly walked back — underscored the cartel's fragility even as it grapples with members who cannot meet existing quotas. France's seizure of the Deliver near Sicily makes five Russian shadow fleet boardings and signals that European naval enforcement is escalating, not tapering. Meanwhile, the Moscow refinery's confirmed outage through 2027 deepens Russia's domestic fuel crisis, and the US Army's decision to site critical minerals plants on military bases marks a new phase in the supply chain contest with China — though production is years away.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, AP News, Le Monde/AFP, TradeArabia, Moneycontrol, CryptoBriefing, Gulf Times, FirstPost, OilPrice.com, Yahoo News, Al Jazeera, SupplyChainBrain, UKMTO, Business Standard, NPR, MSN, Financial Review (AFR)
